preFix.title: Fitting gutter guards to improve rainwater collection quality
preFix.subtitle: Keeping leaves and debris out of the gutter to protect the water butt
preFix.excerpt: A water butt collecting from an unguarded gutter fills with leaf debris, bird droppings, and roof tile sediment. Gutter guards, mesh covers or foam inserts fitted into the gutter channel, substantially reduce the debris load reaching the downpipe and water butt. A first-flush diverter intercepts the first 20 to 50 litres of each rain event. Together they produce cleaner water that is less likely to block soaker hoses or clog pump strainers.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAS guidance on non-potable water collection. RHS water harvesting guidance 2024. Manufacturer installation notes for mesh gutter guards (Rooflite, Hedgehog).

content.text: The quality of water in an unguarded water butt varies widely depending on how much debris the roof and gutter shed. A gutter carrying leaves from an overhanging tree fills with a decomposing leaf soup within two weeks of autumn leaf fall. This water has a high tannin and organic content, problematic for seedling irrigation (can suppress germination in seed trays) and hard on small pump impellers. Gutter guards solve the primary input problem; a

Flat mesh guard, a stainless steel or polypropylene mesh panel that clips over the top of the gutter channel. Water passes through the mesh; leaves sit on top and blow away or are cleared periodically. Effective for most leaf types; less effective for pine needles, which can thread through the mesh.

Foam insert (Hedgehog type), a cylindrical brush insert sits inside the gutter channel. Water flows around it; debris accumulates on top of the foam. Requires cleaning every 1 to 2 years. Not suitable for moss or fine sediment (both work into the foam structure).

Hinged flap gutter cover, a solid cover with a thin slot for water entry. Leaves and twigs cannot enter. Very effective but more expensive than mesh-type guards and may be overkill for most domestic water butt installations.
Measure the gutter length and purchase the appropriate mesh. Most mesh systems come in 3 m or 4 m rolls and cut to length with tin snips or heavy scissors.
Clear the gutter of all debris before fitting.
Slide one edge of the mesh under the roof tile edge and clip the other edge to the outer lip of the gutter. The mesh should be slightly angled to encourage debris to slide off rather than accumulate.
At downpipe heads, cut the mesh to the width of the gutter and tuck the edges under the mesh on each side.

The first flush diverter mounts in the downpipe run between the gutter outlet and the water butt. A floating ball valve opens the diverter chamber as water enters; once the chamber is full (20 to 50 litres depending on the model), the ball floats up and closes the diverter, routing subsequent clean water to the butt. The diverter chamber drains slowly through a pinhole so it is empty and ready for the next event. These two measures, gutter guard plus first-flush diverter, reliably produce butt water with a low suspended solids load suitable for most garden uses.
